## Example solutions

### Prompt 1 - improved

> **Role:** You are a project manager writing updates to senior management.
> **Context:** The project is about introducing AI tools in customer service.
> We are halfway through the pilot and are two weeks ahead of plan.
> **Task:** Write a short status update by email.
> **Format:** Maximum five sentences. Use bullet points for key figures.

The improvement gives the model a clear perspective, concrete content to work
with, and a bounded format. The result is an email management can actually use.

### Prompt 2 - improved

> **Role:** You are a communications adviser.
> **Context:** I am presenting results from an AI experiment to department
> managers. They have limited technical background.
> **Task:** Create an outline for five slides.
> **Format:** One heading and three bullet points per slide.

Context about the audience and the format requirement makes the model adapt the
level and scope instead of producing a generic draft.

### Prompt 3 - improved

> **Role:** You are an analyst reading reports for decision-makers.
> **Context:** The report is a 40-page quarterly report on sales development in
> the Nordic region.
> **Task:** Summarize the most important findings and any red flags.
> **Format:** Five bullet points, maximum two sentences per point.

The task shifts from "summarize everything" to "extract what matters for a
decision," which is far more useful.

### Prompt 4 - improved

> **Role:** You are an experienced writer giving constructive feedback.
> **Context:** The text is an internal memo about a new personnel policy. The
> audience is all employees, including people without an HR background.
> **Task:** Give feedback on clarity, tone, and structure.
> **Format:** Three strengths and three concrete improvement suggestions.

The format and task ensure that the feedback is balanced and actionable instead
of vague praise or generic criticism.
